title: What is a Variable?
eli5_summary: A variable is like a labeled box where you store a value
analogy: Imagine your brain is a desk. A variable is a sticky note — the name is the label, the value is what you wrote on it. You can change what's written anytime.
content_html:
Variables store information

In Python, you create a variable by simply writing its name, an equals sign, and a value:

name = 'Alice'
age = 25
height = 1.75

The equals sign (=) means "store this value". You can use the variable anywhere after that.

Key rules

  • Names can contain letters, numbers, and underscores
  • Names CANNOT start with a number
  • Names are case-sensitive: Age and age are different
  • Use underscores for multi-word names: my_name

Python figures out the type of your variable automatically — you don't have to tell it!

diagram_data.code:
graph LR
  A[name = 'Alice'] --> B[📦 Memory Box]
  B --> C[Value: 'Alice']

title: Data Types: Numbers, Strings & Booleans
eli5_summary: Python has different types of values — numbers, words, and true/false
analogy: Think of data types like different kinds of containers: a box for numbers, a bag for text, and a light switch for yes/no.
content_html:
The 4 Basic Types

TypeExampleWhat it's for
int42Whole numbers
float3.14Decimal numbers
str'hello'Text (strings)
boolTrueTrue or False

Checking the type

x = 42
print(type(x))  # <class 'int'>

y = 'hello'
print(type(y))  # <class 'str'>

Converting types

age = int('25')    # string → int
price = float('9.99')  # string → float
name = str(42)    # int → string

title: Working with Strings
eli5_summary: Strings are text — you can join them, slice them, and ask questions about them
analogy: Strings are like a necklace of beads — each bead is a character. You can cut the necklace, glue two together, or count the beads.
content_html:
String Operations

greeting = 'Hello'
name = 'World'

# Joining (concatenation)
message = greeting + ', ' + name + '!'
print(message)  # Hello, World!

# Length
print(len(message))  # 13

# Slicing (getting part of a string)
print(message[0])    # H  (first character)
print(message[0:5])  # Hello
print(message[-1])   # !  (last character)

Useful String Methods

text = '  hello world  '
print(text.upper())    # '  HELLO WORLD  '
print(text.strip())    # 'hello world'
print(text.replace('world', 'Python'))  # '  hello Python  '
print(text.split())    # ['hello', 'world']

f-strings (the modern way)

name = 'Alice'
age = 25
print(f'My name is {name} and I am {age} years old.')

title: Getting Input from the User
eli5_summary: Use input() to ask the user a question and store what they type
analogy: input() is like a postal box — you put a label on it (the question), and the user drops their answer in. You then open the box and use what's inside.
content_html:
The input() Function

name = input('What is your name? ')
print(f'Hello, {name}!')

Important: input() always returns a string. If you need a number, convert it:

age_str = input('How old are you? ')
age = int(age_str)  # convert string to integer
print(f'In 10 years you will be {age + 10}')

One-liner version

age = int(input('Your age: '))

title: Variables Mini-Project: Name Card Generator
eli5_summary: Build a program that creates a personalized name card using variables
analogy: You're a printer — the user gives you data, you print a beautiful card.
content_html:
Your First Mini-Project!

You'll build a Name Card Generator that asks for the user's name and age, then prints a formatted card.

What you'll use

  • input() — get name and age from user
  • int() — convert age to a number
  • f-strings — format the output
  • String methods — .upper(), .center()

Expected output

============================
      ALICE SMITH       
         Age: 25         
============================

title: Making Decisions with if/else
eli5_summary: if/else lets your program choose different paths based on conditions
analogy: Think of if/else like a fork in the road. Your program reads a sign (the condition) and takes the left or right path.
content_html:
if / elif / else

temperature = 30

if temperature > 25:
    print('It is hot! 🌞')
elif temperature > 15:
    print('It is warm 😊')
else:
    print('Grab a jacket! 🧥')

Comparison Operators

OperatorMeaning
==equals
!=not equals
>greater than
<less than
>=greater or equal
<=less or equal

Combining Conditions

age = 20
has_id = True

if age >= 18 and has_id:
    print('Welcome!')
else:
    print('Sorry, cannot enter.')

diagram_data.code:
graph TD
  A{condition?} -->|True| B[do this]
  A -->|False| C[do that]

title: For Loops: Repeat with Style
eli5_summary: A for loop runs the same code for each item in a sequence
analogy: A for loop is like a conveyor belt — each item passes in front of you one by one, and you do the same thing to each.
content_html:
The for Loop

fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

for fruit in fruits:
    print(f'I love {fruit}!')

range() — loop a number of times

for i in range(5):
    print(i)  # prints 0, 1, 2, 3, 4

for i in range(1, 6):
    print(i)  # prints 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Loop with index

names = ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie']
for i, name in enumerate(names):
    print(f'{i+1}. {name}')

title: While Loops: Keep Going Until...
eli5_summary: A while loop repeats as long as a condition is true
analogy: A while loop is like eating chips — you keep eating WHILE there are chips left, and you stop when the bag is empty.
content_html:
The while Loop

count = 0
while count < 5:
    print(f'Count: {count}')
    count += 1

print('Done!')

Break and Continue

# break exits the loop immediately
for i in range(10):
    if i == 5:
        break
    print(i)  # prints 0 1 2 3 4

# continue skips to the next iteration
for i in range(10):
    if i % 2 == 0:
        continue
    print(i)  # prints 1 3 5 7 9

Infinite loop (careful!)

while True:
    answer = input('Type quit to exit: ')
    if answer == 'quit':
        break
    print(f'You typed: {answer}')

title: What is a Function?
eli5_summary: A function is a named block of code you can run again and again
analogy: A function is like a recipe card — you write the steps once, give it a name, and you can make the dish as many times as you want by just saying the recipe name.
content_html:
Defining and Calling Functions

def greet(name):
    print(f'Hello, {name}! 👋')

greet('Alice')  # Hello, Alice! 👋
greet('Bob')    # Hello, Bob! 👋

Return values

def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(3, 4)
print(result)  # 7

Default parameters

def greet(name, greeting='Hello'):
    print(f'{greeting}, {name}!')

greet('Alice')           # Hello, Alice!
greet('Bob', 'Hi there') # Hi there, Bob!

diagram_data.code:
graph LR
  A[Call greet('Alice')] --> B[def greet(name)]
  B --> C[print Hello Alice]
  C --> D[Return to caller]

title: Lists: Ordered Collections
eli5_summary: A list is an ordered collection of items that you can change
analogy: A list is like a shopping cart — items are in order, you can add things, remove things, and look at any item by its position.
content_html:
Creating and Using Lists

fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

# Accessing items (index starts at 0)
print(fruits[0])   # apple
print(fruits[-1])  # cherry (last item)

# Adding items
fruits.append('mango')      # add to end
fruits.insert(1, 'blueberry')  # add at position

# Removing items
fruits.remove('banana')  # by value
popped = fruits.pop()    # remove and return last

# Useful operations
print(len(fruits))       # count items
print('apple' in fruits) # check membership
fruits.sort()            # sort in place

title: Dictionaries: Key-Value Stores
eli5_summary: A dictionary stores data as key-value pairs — like a real dictionary with words and definitions
analogy: A dictionary is like a phone book — you look up a name (the key) and get a phone number (the value).
content_html:
Creating and Using Dictionaries

person = {
    'name': 'Alice',
    'age': 25,
    'city': 'Paris'
}

# Accessing values
print(person['name'])        # Alice
print(person.get('age'))     # 25
print(person.get('email', 'N/A'))  # N/A (default)

# Modifying
person['age'] = 26           # update
person['email'] = 'a@b.com'  # add new key
del person['city']           # delete key

# Iterating
for key, value in person.items():
    print(f'{key}: {value}')

title: Classes & Objects
eli5_summary: A class is a blueprint; an object is the actual thing built from that blueprint
analogy: A class is like a cookie cutter — the cutter is the class (the shape/design), and each cookie you make is an object (an instance).
content_html:
Defining a Class

class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, breed):
        self.name = name
        self.breed = breed

    def bark(self):
        print(f'{self.name} says: Woof! 🐶')

    def describe(self):
        print(f'{self.name} is a {self.breed}')

# Creating objects
fido = Dog('Fido', 'Labrador')
rex = Dog('Rex', 'Poodle')

fido.bark()      # Fido says: Woof!
rex.describe()   # Rex is a Poodle

__init__ is the constructor

It runs automatically when you create a new object. self refers to the object being created.

title: Reading and Writing Files
eli5_summary: Python can read from and write to files on your computer
analogy: Opening a file in Python is like opening a notebook — you can read what's written, or open it to write new stuff.
content_html:
Writing a File

with open('notes.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('Hello, file!\n')
    f.write('Second line.')

Reading a File

with open('notes.txt', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()
    print(content)

# Read line by line
with open('notes.txt', 'r') as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.strip())

File Modes

  • 'r' — read (default)
  • 'w' — write (overwrites existing!)
  • 'a' — append (adds to existing)
  • 'r+' — read and write

Always use with open(...) — it automatically closes the file!

title: Handling Errors with try/except
eli5_summary: try/except lets you catch errors so your program doesn't crash
analogy: try/except is like a safety net under a tightrope walker — if they fall (an error happens), the net catches them instead of letting them hit the ground.
content_html:
try / except

try:
    number = int(input('Enter a number: '))
    result = 10 / number
    print(f'Result: {result}')
except ValueError:
    print('That was not a number!')
except ZeroDivisionError:
    print('Cannot divide by zero!')
except Exception as e:
    print(f'Something went wrong: {e}')
finally:
    print('This always runs.')

Common Exception Types

  • ValueError — wrong type of value
  • TypeError — operation on wrong type
  • FileNotFoundError — file doesn't exist
  • ZeroDivisionError — dividing by zero
  • KeyError — dictionary key missing
  • IndexError — list index out of range

title: What is an API?
eli5_summary: An API is a messenger that carries requests to other systems and brings back results
analogy: An API is like a waiter at a restaurant. You (your code) tell the waiter (API) what you want. The waiter goes to the kitchen (the server), gets your food (data), and brings it back to you.
content_html:
APIs connect programs

An API (Application Programming Interface) lets your Python code talk to other services — like getting weather data, searching for movies, or sending messages.

Making HTTP Requests with requests

import requests

# GET request — fetching data
response = requests.get('https://api.github.com/users/python')

print(response.status_code)  # 200 = success
data = response.json()        # parse JSON response
print(data['name'])           # Python

Status Codes

  • 200 — OK (success)
  • 404 — Not Found
  • 401 — Unauthorized
  • 500 — Server Error

diagram_data.code:
sequenceDiagram
  Your Code->>API: GET /weather
  API->>Server: fetch data
  Server->>API: JSON response
  API->>Your Code: data

title: Project: Number Guessing Game
eli5_summary: Build a complete game where the computer picks a number and the player guesses it
analogy: You're building a magic trick — the computer is the magician who picked a secret number, and the user tries to read its mind.
content_html:
Your First Complete Project!

You'll build a Number Guessing Game using everything you've learned:

  • Variables — store the secret number and guess count
  • random module — generate random numbers
  • while loops — keep asking until correct
  • if/elif/else — give hints (too high / too low)
  • input() — get the player's guess
  • Functions — organize the code

Features to build

  1. Computer picks a random number 1–100
  2. Player guesses until correct
  3. Give feedback: 'Too high!' or 'Too low!'
  4. Count the number of guesses
  5. Congratulate with their score
